tools.name: query
tools.description:
Query for objects in ECHO.
Use this tool when searching for information in the space (both automerge and queues).
Currently, two types of queries are supported:
  - Full-text search - terms may appear anywhere in the object.
  - Type-based search - objects of a specific type.
You can use them together, for example, to search for objects of a specific type that match a full-text query.
Important: Whem querying by typename, make sure to list the schema first, to get the exact typename.

Omit both typename and text to search for all objects.

<output_format>
  You can choose to either return the full object data, or just the DXN, type and label.
  When expecting a lot of results, run with includeContent=false to not pollute the context, and then load specific objects using the load tool.

  You can choose to get the content right away, if you don't expect a lot of results.
  To load content right away, run with includeContent=true.
  When loading content, set an appropriate limit on the number of results to avoid overwhelming the context.
</output_format>

<example description="All tasks related to Cyberdyne and Bob">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.task",
    "text": "cyberdyne bob",
  }
</example>

<example description="Financial report Q1 2026">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.document",
    "text": "financial report Q1 2026",
    "includeContent": true
    "limit": 3
  }
</example>

<example description="Emails from specific mailboxes">
  {
    "in": [{"/" : "dxn:echo:@:YYYYYY"}, {"/" : "dxn:echo:@:XXXXXXX"}],
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.email",
    "includeContent": true,
    "limit": 20
  }
</example>

in.description: Scope the query to children of specific objects (transitively). Use this to query items within containers such as feeds or folders. For example, to find emails use `in: [mailbox1.feed, mailbox2.feed]`.

typename.description: The typename of the objects to list.

includeContent.description: Include the full object data in the response.

limit.description: The maximum number of results to return.

includeQueues.description: Search in queues as well as spaces. Only use this if searching for emails.

tools.name: query
tools.description:
Query for objects in ECHO.
Use this tool when searching for information in the space (both automerge and queues).
Currently, two types of queries are supported:
  - Full-text search - terms may appear anywhere in the object.
  - Type-based search - objects of a specific type.
You can use them together, for example, to search for objects of a specific type that match a full-text query.
Important: Whem querying by typename, make sure to list the schema first, to get the exact typename.

Omit both typename and text to search for all objects.

<output_format>
  You can choose to either return the full object data, or just the DXN, type and label.
  When expecting a lot of results, run with includeContent=false to not pollute the context, and then load specific objects using the load tool.

  You can choose to get the content right away, if you don't expect a lot of results.
  To load content right away, run with includeContent=true.
  When loading content, set an appropriate limit on the number of results to avoid overwhelming the context.
</output_format>

<example description="All tasks related to Cyberdyne and Bob">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.task",
    "text": "cyberdyne bob",
  }
</example>

<example description="Financial report Q1 2026">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.document",
    "text": "financial report Q1 2026",
    "includeContent": true
    "limit": 3
  }
</example>

<example description="Emails from specific mailboxes">
  {
    "in": [{"/" : "dxn:echo:@:YYYYYY"}, {"/" : "dxn:echo:@:XXXXXXX"}],
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.email",
    "includeContent": true,
    "limit": 20
  }
</example>

typename.description: The typename of the objects to list.

includeContent.description: Include the full object data in the response.

limit.description: The maximum number of results to return.

includeQueues.description: Search in queues as well as spaces. Only use this if searching for emails.

Currently, two types of queries are supported:
  - Full-text search - terms may appear anywhere in the object.
  - Type-based search - objects of a specific type.
You can use them together, for example, to search for objects of a specific type that match a full-text query.
Important: Whem querying by typename, make sure to list the schema first, to get the exact typename.

Omit both typename and text to search for all objects.

<output_format>
  You can choose to either return the full object data, or just the DXN, type and label.
  When expecting a lot of results, run with includeContent=false to not pollute the context, and then load specific objects using the load tool.

  You can choose to get the content right away, if you don't expect a lot of results.
  To load content right away, run with includeContent=true.
  When loading content, set an appropriate limit on the number of results to avoid overwhelming the context.
</output_format>

  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.task",
    "text": "cyberdyne bob",
  }
</example>

  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.document",
    "text": "financial report Q1 2026",
    "includeContent": true

  }
</example>

    "limit": 20
  }
</example>

tools.name: query
tools.description:
Query for objects in ECHO.
Use this tool when searching for information in the space (both automerge and queues).
Currently, two types of queries are supported:
  - Full-text search - terms may appear anywhere in the object.
  - Type-based search - objects of a specific type.
You can use them together, for example, to search for objects of a specific type that match a full-text query.
Important: Whem querying by typename, make sure to list the schema first, to get the exact typename.

Omit both typename and text to search for all objects.

<output_format>
  You can choose to either return the full object data, or just the DXN, type and label.
  When expecting a lot of results, run with includeContent=false to not pollute the context, and then load specific objects using the load tool.

  You can choose to get the content right away, if you don't expect a lot of results.
  To load content right away, run with includeContent=true.
  When loading content, set an appropriate limit on the number of results to avoid overwhelming the context.
</output_format>

<example description="All tasks related to Cyberdyne and Bob">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.task",
    "text": "cyberdyne bob",
  }
</example>

<example description="Financial report Q1 2026">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.document",
    "text": "financial report Q1 2026",
    "includeContent": true
    "limit": 3
  }
</example>

<example description="Emails from specific mailboxes">
  {
    "in": [{"/" : "dxn:echo:@:YYYYYY"}, {"/" : "dxn:echo:@:XXXXXXX"}],
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.email",
    "includeContent": true,
    "limit": 20
  }
</example>

in.description: Scope the query to children of specific objects (transitively). Use this to query items within containers such as feeds or folders. For example, to find emails use `in: [mailbox1.feed, mailbox2.feed]`.

typename.description: The typename of the objects to list.

includeContent.description: Include the full object data in the response.

limit.description: The maximum number of results to return.

includeQueues.description: Search in queues as well as spaces. Only use this if searching for emails.

# DXOS — Research Summary

## Overview

<cite index="1-3">DXOS is an open source framework for building real-time, collaborative web applications that run entirely on the client and communicate peer-to-peer, without the need for centralized servers.</cite> The name stands for **Decentralized Operating System**. <cite index="9-3">DXOS is an independent public organization whose mission is to provide open source technologies that allow for a free and open decentralized internet.</cite>

## Company Background

<cite index="21-3,21-4">DXOS was founded in 2021 and is headquartered in New York, NY.</cite> <cite index="21-6">DXOS is a private company</cite> <cite index="28-1">with 11-50 employees.</cite> <cite index="8-6">DXOS was created by a small core team of developers from many countries.</cite> <cite index="9-4">DXOS.org is a decentralized global community of open source developers.</cite>

### Investors & Funding

<cite index="21-1">Backed VC, BlueYard Capital, Empty Set Group, and PL Capital (United States) have invested in DXOS.</cite>

## Mission & Vision

<cite index="18-4">DXOS's mission is to help developers build privacy-preserving, self-sovereign software.</cite> <cite index="1-21,1-22">They are setting a new standard for how modern software is built by reducing reliance on centralized servers and focusing on scalable peer-to-peer data networks. Their framework empowers anyone to create real-time collaborative tools within a privacy-focused, local-first workspace that is enhanced by AI capabilities.</cite>

<cite index="2-3,2-4">DXOS is described as a developer of a decentralized platform designed to create the first functionally and commercially competitive, fully decentralized alternative to the corporate cloud. The company offers a network operating system, infrastructure platform, programming framework, and toolchain, enabling businesses to develop privacy-preserving applications.</cite>

## Core Technology & Architecture

DXOS is built around three core protocol layers:

### 1. ECHO (Data Layer)
<cite index="1-6">ECHO is an open source database architecture that incorporates transparent data replication and conflict resolution for secure and scalable local storage.</cite> <cite index="10-10">It serves as a peer-to-peer database and reactive state container for offline-first, real-time, collaborative apps.</cite>

### 2. HALO (Identity Layer)
<cite index="1-7">HALO seamlessly manages digital identities using public/private key pairs allowing nuanced control over access to applications, databases, and networked devices.</cite> <cite index="5-5">HALO is a decentralized identity and access control protocol that makes it easier to build collaborative applications.</cite>

### 3. MESH (Networking Layer)
<cite index="1-9">MESH extends existing internet protocols to enable secure and resilient peer-to-peer networks without the need for centralized server infrastructure.</cite> <cite index="1-19">It automatically synchronizes data across networked devices using WebRTC for cloudless peer-to-peer networking.</cite>

### Underlying CRDT Technology
<cite index="1-11,1-12">DXOS leverages Automerge — a powerful CRDT that enables multiple concurrent users to operate on the same data without loss or conflict.</cite>

### How It Works
<cite index="1-14,1-15">The only reason you need a central server is to serve your application shell. Everything else happens in the client and works peer-to-peer.</cite> <cite index="5-6">There are still signaling servers for peer discovery, but all data exchange happens peer-to-peer, and no servers have access to the data.</cite>

## Developer SDK & Tools

<cite index="3-1">DXOS is the developer platform for collaborative, local-first, privacy-preserving software.</cite> <cite index="3-5,3-6">DXOS works in any Node.js or Browser environment. There is a TypeScript API and a React API.</cite>

<cite index="20-11">It consists of three main components wrapped together in a set of SDKs (currently, React and TypeScript) that enable developers to deliver sophisticated applications quickly.</cite>

<cite index="3-27,3-28">DXOS recommends Vite as the bundler. Vite requires a plugin in order to serve the WebAssembly modules.</cite>

The SDK is available on npm under `@dxos/client` and `@dxos/react-client`.

## Flagship Application: Composer

<cite index="34-1">Composer is a collaborative productivity app where developers can build and organize knowledge, extend with custom data and UI, and run private AI against their knowledge locally.</cite> <cite index="18-3">Composer is an open source "Super App" that provides teams with a fully customizable and extensible environment in a single integrated workspace.</cite>

### Composer Features
<cite index="31-16">Composer blends the power of popular file formats (docs, sheets, sketch & tables) while allowing you to build plugins, deploy custom functions and leverage modern LLMs.</cite>

Key capabilities include:
- <cite index="31-17">Real-time collaboration inside a modern markdown editor.</cite>
- <cite index="31-19">Threaded comments across multiple document formats.</cite>
- <cite index="31-21">Custom plugins to enhance functionality.</cite>
- <cite index="31-23">Custom schemas to work with structured data.</cite>
- <cite index="31-25">A local-first spreadsheet that supports custom functions.</cite>
- <cite index="31-26">Embedded diagrams and sketches for programmatic workflows.</cite>
- <cite index="31-29">Machine learning to build agentic workflows that enhance team productivity while protecting intellectual property.</cite>
- <cite index="31-36">Custom agents that use Composer data as a dynamic context window.</cite>
- <cite index="31-51">Import and export of the entire database as plain, human-readable markdown, JSON, and media files.</cite>

### Composer Architecture
<cite index="37-3,37-4">Composer is built with extensibility at its core. Everything within the application is built using the same App Framework APIs, from the UI down to data and business logic — anything in Composer can be customized and enhanced.</cite>

### Privacy by Design
<cite index="14-7,14-8">Composer is built on the peer-to-peer protocols of DXOS which are designed to be private by default. The data is stored on your device and only shared with others you explicitly invite.</cite> <cite index="31-1,31-2">You own everything inside of Composer. No spyware, no algorithms, no lock-in.</cite>

### Current Status
<cite index="34-2,34-3">Composer is not yet ready for production. There are bugs, and breaking changes may occur.</cite> <cite index="5-2">DXOS is launching a fully-managed service to host agents.</cite>

## Community & Open Source

<cite index="1-23">The DXOS Community is made of a diverse group of open source developers from all over the world.</cite> <cite index="31-7">The community spans 8 cities worldwide, has 30+ contributors and 200+ community members.</cite> <cite index="4-2">DXOS has 64 repositories available on GitHub.</cite> The project is licensed under the MIT license.

## Key Differentiators from Traditional SaaS

- **Local-first**: Data is stored and processed on the user's device, not in the cloud
- **Peer-to-peer**: Communication happens directly between devices without centralized servers
- **Privacy-preserving**: No server ever has access to user data
- **Offline-capable**: Applications work fully offline with automatic sync upon reconnection
- **Open source**: Full transparency and the ability to self-host
- **CRDT-based**: Conflict-free data synchronization for real-time collaboration
- **Extensible**: Plugin architecture for customizable applications

## Links

- **Website**: https://dxos.org
- **Documentation**: https://docs.dxos.org
- **GitHub**: https://github.com/dxos/dxos
- **Blog**: https://blog.dxos.org
- **Composer**: https://dxos.org/composer
